title: Continuous Reset-Induced Phase Transition in Measurement-Free Random Quantum Circuits
authors: Hinata Yokoyama, Kengo Anzai, Dina Syverud-Lindland, Yoshihito Kuno, Hiroaki Matsueda
abstract: We study a random unitary quantum circuit with only reset channels, which has high feasibility for real quantum devices. In particular, we investigate the many-body statistical physics properties, "reset-induced" entanglement phase transitions comparing the classical statistical picture in the large "$d$" limit of qudits. In the property of the reset-induced phase transition the parameter of qudit $d$ is essential. That is, the transition properties induced by the reset channel significantly depend on $d$. We numerically elucidate this statement employing efficient stabilizer circuit simulations for $d=2$. Specifically, large fluctuations are observed near the critical point, indicating that the reset-induced phase transition is continuous. We obtain clear data collapses, consistent with a second-order mixed phase transition. This behavior differs from expectations based on the classical statistical mapping in the large-$d$ limit.
summary: This paper explores phase transitions in random quantum circuits driven by reset channels rather than measurements. While previous studies in the large-dimension limit predicted a first-order transition, this work uses stabilizer simulations to show that for qubits, the transition is continuous and follows the 2D Ising universality class. The study also demonstrates that the critical point depends on the ratio of circuit depth to system size. These findings provide a deeper understanding of how local dissipation shapes entanglement structure in quantum many-body systems.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of entanglement phase transitions induced by reset channels in random quantum circuits, specifically determining if the transition is continuous or first-order in the qubit regime.
main_result: For qubits (d=2), the reset-induced phase transition is found to be continuous (second-order) and belongs to the 2D Ising universality class, contradicting the first-order transition predicted by the large-d classical mapping.
method: The authors use efficient stabilizer circuit simulations for d=2 and an analytical mapping of the circuit onto a 2D classical statistical spin model on a triangular lattice.
model_or_system: A 1D random Clifford (unitary) quantum circuit with a brickwork pattern of two-site gates, subject to a local reset channel that purifies sites to the |0> state with probability q.
key_observables: Logarithmic purity (Sp), Many-body negativity (MBN/En), entanglement entropy, and the variance of these observables.
important_parameters: Qudit dimension (d), reset probability (p or q), and the ratio of total time to system size (T/L).
main_assumptions: The large-d limit assumes a mapping to a classical statistical spin model; the study focuses on the d=2 (qubit) regime using stabilizer formalism.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the circuit schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the p-dependence of purity and its variance, demonstrating data collapse for finite-size scaling; Appendix figures show the evolution of spin configurations and domain walls.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the reset-induced transition, describes the circuit model and its mapping to a classical spin model, presents numerical results for d=2 showing a continuous transition, discusses the dependence on T/L, and concludes with the comparison between small-d and large-d regimes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it identifies a new universality class for noise-induced transitions and explores how the dimension of the Hilbert space fundamentally changes the order of a phase transition.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A schematic representation of the random Clifford circuit subjected to the reset channel. The orange block represents a two- site random Clifford unitary gate, and the blue dot represents a reset channel. The horizontal and vertical axes represent the spatial and time directions, respectively. The reset channel is applied at each site with probability q = p/L. The system evolves from product states of |0⟩basis. Each time step includes two layer of the two-site unitary gates and the single layer reset channel with probability q for each sites.

FIG. 2. (a) p-dependence of Sp. The data were taken from L = 128 to L = 256. The dashed lines represent analytical expectation of Eq. (7) of p < pc case. (b) p-dependence of variance of Sp. The peak increases as L is incremented. Inset: The data of FSS. We observe the data collapse. ν = 1.04(36) and pc = 0.20(3) are obtained. For all data point, the trajectory samples are O(103) and the total time step is T = 4L.

FIG. 3. (a) p-dependence of the MBN. The lines for different system sizes from L = 128 to L = 256 merge all together after p ≈0.26 (inset). (b) The results of the FSS for the MBN. The numerically extracted results are pc = 0.268(12) and ν = 2. (c) L-dependence of EN and the linear fitting for p = 0.20. The data were averaged over O(103) at T = 4L.

FIG. 4. p-dependence of variance of EN. The peak increases with the increase of L. Inset: The data of FSS analysis. We observe the data collapse with ν = 1.08(16) and pc = 0.20(3). The total number of the trajectory sample is O(103) and we set T = 4L.
FIG. 5. p-dependence of the second derivative for Sp [(a)] and EN [(b)]. Both peaks are observed with p = 0.24. The absolute values of the peaks get large as increasing the system size L.

FIG. 7. Transition-boundary curve of the reset-induced phase tran- sition on T/L-p plane. This result is obtained by observing the log- arithmic purity and the MBN. The curve is created by numerically estimated critical points pc’s. The blue (orange) line is the estimated curve using Sp (EN). The regions above and below the curve corre- spond to distinct phases associated with each quantity. The numeri- cally extracted results are ν ≈1 for Sp and ν = 2 for the MBN. The data is taken at T/L = 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, and 40. The fitting curve suggests that T/L ∝1/pc.

FIG. 6. T/L-dependence of Sp [(a)] and EN [(b)].We fix p = 0.25. Both values saturate to a finite value at T ∼4L in which the reset-induced phase transition occurs. The data were averaged over O(103).
